text: The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection over the sacral promontory, preparing the surgical field for the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a challenging total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, navigating beneath the adhesions to the uterus, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a standard medial to lateral dissection, carefully maneuvering underneath the visible superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon exposes the iliac vessels and the ureter, highlighting their anatomical positions in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the ureter in the surgical field, noting its position without the use of stents during the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to isolate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, focusing on the area where the artery is visually identifiable.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform a faster dissection, focusing on coning in onto the superior hemorrhoid wall.
The surgeon visually identifies the takeoff of the left colic artery and chooses to ligate the superior hemorrhoidal artery rather than the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, carefully taking a portion of the mesentery before proceeding to cone in for the total mesorectal excision (TME).
The ureter is visible in the surgical field, identified and exposed during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) in the posterior location, carefully dropping the gonadal vessels and ureter to proceed with the dissection.
The surgeon performs a limited lateral dissection to assist in joining the surgical plane, focusing on the visible tissue manipulation in the area.
Adhesions from a previous myomectomy are visible near the uterus, as the surgeon navigates the surgical field during the colectomy.
The surgeon highlights the normal white line along the descending colon, a visible anatomical landmark during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon proceeds to join the medial dissection, aligning with the previously established surgical planes.
A sponge pad is briefly visible underneath the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the left-sided total mesorectal excision (TME), focusing on the dissection of the mesorectal tissue on the left side.
The surgeon carefully inspects the area to ensure the ureter is not being drawn up, then begins addressing the adhesions to the uterus that are obstructing the surgical field.
Dense adhesions from a prior surgery are visible, obstructing the surgical field. The surgeon creates a window into the deeper pelvis to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon performs posterior total mesorectal excision (TME), followed by right lateral dissection, while visually checking the ureter to ensure it is not involved in the surgical field.

The surgeon addresses anterior adhesions to the uterus and cervix, using an arm for elevation. After further dissection, the uterus is suspended with a Keith needle nylon suture, freeing up the arm for continued manipulation.

The surgeon performs left lateral total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, checking the ureter's position. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence is used to enhance visualization and speed up the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies a green spot, which is the ICG tattoo, marking the distal extent of the dissection area.

The surgeon uses ICG intraoperatively to identify the distal extent of the dissection, marked approximately one to two centimeters distal to the tumor. The tattoo is visible as the surgeon continues posterior dissection.
The surgeon prepares for anterior dissection between the rectum and vagina, focusing on the right side of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the ICG tattoos located just beneath the peritoneal reflection, indicating the area for dissection. The procedure progresses towards creating a coloanal anastomosis at the level of the levator muscles.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision (TME), removing the rectal mesentery within a wide pelvic cavity. Adhesions to the uterus have been previously taken down, facilitating this part of the dissection.
The surgeon works circumferentially to dissect below the green ICG tattoo, revealing the vagina in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection using scissors, focusing on the lateral vessels entering the mesorectum, rather than switching to a vessel sealer.

The surgeon continues the dissection to remove the mesorectum, starting on the right side and joining the anterior dissection between the rectum and vagina. The same process is repeated on the left side, ensuring complete removal of the mesorectal tissue.

The surgeon continues the dissection, using scissors to carefully navigate around the iliac vessels. The camera is positioned on arm two, providing a straight shot view as the dissection progresses down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon removes the complete complement of the mesorectum from the pelvis, following the holy plane. The right-sided portion of the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection is completed, and the surgeon prepares to perform the same dissection on the left side.
The surgeon follows the dissection plane, clearly visible in this patient, to complete the dissection on the left side. The tattoo marking is visible above the dissection area.
The surgeon completes the posterior portion of the total mesorectal excision (TME) down to the pelvic floor, with the camera repositioned in arm three for optimal visualization.

The surgeon proceeds to take down the flexure, with the ICG tattoos visible well above the dissection site. The setup remains consistent with the previous lower dissection, utilizing the same port configuration.
The pancreas is visible as the surgeon completes the dissection around the area until the flexure is fully mobilized. The surgeon then prepares to staple the distal section.
The surgeon performs a stapled anastomosis, exteriorizing the bowel through a Pfannenstiel incision.
The camera view shows the complete total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection with the staple line visible nestled in the pelvic diaphragm beneath the clearly visible levator muscles.

The surgeon inserts the anvil laparoscopically, ensuring the stapler's outer ring is positioned safely away from the vagina. The stapler is used to complete the anastomosis, providing a clear view of the surgical field.

The surgeon instructs the physician assistant at the bedside to fill the pelvis and compress the conduit against the sacrum. The anastomosis is visibly wide open, located at the level of the inter-rectal ring, with no bubbles present.
